source: Compiled from SABR, University of Baltimore, SHSMO, NLBM, NPR, PBS documentation
methodology: Index compiled from institutional collection descriptions, catalog records, and published references. Audio not accessed directly. Metadata verified against institutional documentation where available. Durations for SABR interviews verified from sabr.org catalog pages. University of Baltimore collection verified from ArchivesSpace finding aid. SHSMO K0047 details from published collection descriptions. NPR interviews verified from npr.org archive. Ken Burns Baseball participants verified from PBS documentation and Wikipedia.

gaps.name: Josh Gibson
gaps.note: Died January 20, 1947, three months before Jackie Robinson's major league debut. Before systematic oral history collection began. No known recorded interviews survive.
gaps.secondary: Substantial indirect testimony through contemporaries. Buck O'Neil, Monte Irvin, and others describe Gibson at length in their recorded interviews.
gaps.source: Wikipedia: Josh Gibson died January 20, 1947 of a stroke. No audio recordings documented.

gaps.name: Oscar Charleston
gaps.note: Died October 5, 1954. No known substantial oral history recordings. The most complete account of his career comes through newspaper reporting and the recollections of contemporaries recorded decades later.
gaps.secondary: Secondary testimony exists in the Fay Vincent interview series and in SABR oral histories from players who saw him play.
gaps.source: Wikipedia: Oscar Charleston died October 5, 1954. No documented recordings.

gaps.name: Rube Foster
gaps.note: Died December 9, 1930 in Kankakee, Illinois. Founded the Negro National League. Institutionalized from 1926 until his death. No audio recording technology was widely available during his active career.
gaps.secondary: No direct testimony. Career documented through newspaper accounts. Voice represented by actor Delroy Lindo in Ken Burns Baseball (1994).

gaps.name: Cristobal Torriente
gaps.note: Died April 11, 1938 in New York City. Decades before any organized oral history collection of Negro Leagues players. The earliest surviving recordings postdate his death by forty years.
gaps.secondary: No direct testimony. Career documented through newspaper accounts and secondary historical sources.

gaps.name: Smokey Joe Williams
gaps.note: Died February 25, 1951 in New York City. No known recorded interviews.
gaps.secondary: No direct testimony documented. Career reconstructed from newspaper accounts.
gaps.source: Wikipedia: Smokey Joe Williams died February 25, 1951.

gaps.name: Pop Lloyd
gaps.note: Died March 19, 1964 in Atlantic City. Despite living into the 1960s, no oral history recordings have been documented. His widow was interviewed for the book 'Only the Ball Was White' but no audio of Lloyd himself is known.
gaps.secondary: Widow interviewed by Robert Peterson for 'Only the Ball Was White.' No direct audio testimony.

gaps.name: Turkey Stearnes
gaps.note: Died September 4, 1979 in Detroit. Lived into the era of oral history collection but few documented recordings exist. The gap documents what was not prioritized.
gaps.secondary: Limited secondary testimony. Brief mentions in contemporaries' interviews. Historian John B. Holway conducted interviews that captured some of Stearnes' quotes.
gaps.source: Wikipedia: Turkey Stearnes died September 4, 1979. Holway interviews referenced.

gaps.name: Mule Suttles
gaps.note: Died July 9, 1966 in Newark, New Jersey. Before the major oral history collection campaigns of the 1970s and 1980s. No known recorded interviews.
gaps.secondary: Secondary testimony through players who competed against him, primarily in SABR collection interviews from the 1990s. Lenny Pearson recollections in John Holway's 'Blackball Stars.'

gaps.name: Willie Wells
gaps.note: Died January 22, 1989 in Austin, Texas. Despite living well into the oral history era, no documented recordings have been located.
gaps.secondary: No documented direct audio testimony. Career reconstructed from newspaper accounts and secondary sources.

gaps.name: Ray Dandridge
gaps.note: Died February 12, 1994 in Palm Bay, Florida. Inducted into the Hall of Fame in 1987. No documented oral history recordings have been located despite living into the 1990s.
gaps.secondary: Career documented primarily through newspaper accounts and secondary historical sources.

gaps.name: Bullet Rogan
gaps.note: Died March 4, 1967 in Kansas City. Before the SHSMO oral history campaign that began in 1978. No known recorded interviews.
gaps.secondary: Contemporaries' recollections in later interviews provide indirect testimony.

gaps.name: Hilton Smith
gaps.note: Died November 18, 1983 in Kansas City. One SHSMO interview from 1978 is documented in the K0047 collection, but this represents minimal recording relative to his historical significance.
gaps.secondary: The 1978 SHSMO interview is the only confirmed recording. Additional testimony may exist in NLBM archives.
gaps.source: Wikipedia: Hilton Smith died November 18, 1983. SHSMO K0047 lists an interview.

gaps.name: Leon Day
gaps.note: Died March 13, 1995 in Baltimore, six days after being elected to the Hall of Fame. Limited documented recordings relative to his significance.
gaps.secondary: Some SABR oral history material exists but the documented record is thin for a Hall of Famer.

gaps.name: Ray Brown
gaps.note: Died February 8, 1965. Among the greatest pitchers in Negro Leagues history. No known oral history recordings.
gaps.secondary: Brief secondary testimony from Homestead Grays teammates in later recordings.
gaps.source: Historical references. No Wikipedia recording documentation.

gaps.name: Willard Brown
gaps.note: Died August 4, 1996 in Houston, Texas, after suffering from Alzheimer's disease. Despite living into the 1990s, no documented oral history recordings have been located.
gaps.secondary: Buck O'Neil described him as 'the most natural ballplayer I ever saw' in recorded interviews.

institutions.name: SABR Oral History Collection
institutions.fullName: Society for American Baseball Research, Oral History Committee
institutions.note: Largest single collection. Negro Leagues subset browsable by tag. Interviews conducted by Fay Vincent, Dave Paulson, Paul Motyka, Tom Harris, Nicholas Digrispino, and others. Many include PDF transcripts.

institutions.name: University of Baltimore, Langsdale Library Special Collections
institutions.accessLabel: Open, digitized. 7 audio recordings and all 8 transcripts available.
institutions.note: Conducted in 1998. Digitized by Aiden Faust, Fatemeh Rezaei, Laura Bell. Interviewees: Ernest Burke, Mamie Johnson, Robert Hieronimus, Gordon Hopkins, Robert Leffler Jr., Hubert Simmons, Charles Winner, Geraldine Day. Interviewers: Steve Buckman, Crystal Barnett, Reba Cottingham, Seth Crawford, Harry Cornias, Stacey Lee, Talmadge Ford III.

institutions.name: State Historical Society of Missouri
institutions.fullName: Kansas City Monarchs Oral History Collection (K0047)
institutions.note: Interviews and correspondence, 1978-1981. Focused on Kansas City Monarchs players and associates.

institutions.name: Negro Leagues Baseball Museum
institutions.fullName: NLBM Archive, Buck O'Neil Education and Research Center
institutions.note: Internal archive. Some materials publicly accessible through museum programming.

institutions.name: National Baseball Hall of Fame Library
institutions.fullName: A. Bartlett Giamatti Research Center, Oral History Holdings
institutions.note: Major collection with varying access levels. Collaborates with SABR on digitization.

institutions.name: NPR Archive
institutions.fullName: National Public Radio, Historical interview recordings
institutions.note: Includes Mamie Johnson interviews: Bob Edwards (Morning Edition, Feb 18 2003) and Scott Simon (Weekend Edition Saturday, Aug 31 2002).

institutions.name: PBS / Florentine Films
institutions.note: Full interview footage with Buck O'Neil, Ted Radcliffe, and others. Broadcast excerpts are public; full footage requires institutional access.
